patient_utterances: Hello.
[Clinician asked: "Hello, hi, I'm Doctor Jacob and welcome to Babylon. Hello."] Nice to meet you.
[Clinician asked: "Nice to meet you too. So just before we start, is it all right if you could confirm your name for me please?"] Michael John .
[Clinician asked: "And your date of birth?"] Oh nine two nine eighty-three.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and your address for me please."] Two three one, Leonard, London street uh, sorry, London.
[Clinician asked: "OK and that's correct, and just to confirm that you're in a secure location and we can have a confidential conversation."] Yes.

[Clinician asked: "OK, uh, you've mentioned on our notes that you're suffering from bad diarrhea. I'm sorry to hear about it. So if you tell me a bit more about it, please."] Uh, it's been happening for about, three days now? Um, yeah it, started off uh, was, I don't know it's just something that I thought would go away pretty quickly.

[Clinician asked: "OK. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] I've had that from time to time after eating certain types of food or going on certain travels, but, uh nothing funky in my diet and no travels recently, um and it just kinda kept up. I'm a bit confused as to what to do .

[Clinician asked: "Mmm. Mm-hmm. So you haven't had anything from outside? Nothing dodgy anything ? No."] No, no no no, just a little bit, yeah I I don't know I mean it's pretty standard diarrhoea, where it's was like a little bit of abdominal pain. I threw up once,  that was a little worrying, uh.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Mm-hmm. Repeat that please, that wasn't clear. What did you say?"] I threw up once, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> After that we'd said something."] Uh, nothing else was worrisome.
[Clinician asked: "OK, right, and with the diarrhea, how many times a day are you going to the toilet?"] Uh, four to five.
[Clinician asked: "Four to five times, yeah. And is there any? And it's like water? Is it like water or is it just loose?"] Uh, what do you mean?
[Clinician asked: "Are you going like water? Or is it just, you know bit, bit of  solid stools, bit loose kind of thing?"] It probably depends on the time of day to be honest, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>.
[Clinician asked: "Sure OK. Bit of both and is there any blood or mucus?"] No blood. What do you mean mucus?
[Clinician asked: "It's like the snotty stuff."] In the stool?
[Clinician asked: "Yeah, it looks like snot ."] No.
[Clinician asked: "No, so no mucus, OK. Any fever at all?"] Uh, maybe a little bit, but.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm."] It hasn't been, that in particular hasn't been too worrying.

[Clinician asked: "Are you still able to eat, and drink?"] Uh, a little bit of. Appetite loss I guess worse than normal,  but also not something I've, like I, if that was alone I wouldn't have, reached out.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Sure. So but you're you're still able to drink quite a bit yeah? You're drinking plenty."] Yeah yeah yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Excellent."] Mostly it's been dry and red.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and, um, what have you take, have you taken anything so far for the diarrhea?"] Nothing yet.
[Clinician asked: "Nothing yet, OK. Anything for the abdominal pain?"] Uh, nothing yet.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Right, and any  unfortunately we don't have any of your past medical history, is there anything you'd like to say?"] Uh, I. Probably I  I had asthma.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm."] Um or, or I don't know I guess I still have it.
[Clinician asked: "OK and uh, are you, any, uh, anything else other than asthma?"] Uh, my dad had hypertension.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Has hypertension. Had hypertension.  Yeah he died of bowel cancer ten years ago.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>. Ohh dear, OK. Bowel cancer. Ten years ago. And how old was he when he died?"] Uh, sixty-two.
[Clinician asked: "K, and um, you mentioned you had asthma, are you using any inhalers at all?"] Yes.
[Clinician asked: "Which ones?"] Lexapro.
[Clinician asked: "Sorry?"] Lexapro.
[Clinician asked: "Laxepro."] Yes.
[Clinician asked: "OK, not heard of it may. Is it like Salbutamol? Is it like a blue colour?"] Yellow.

[Clinician asked: "Yellow, OK. I'll have to check that one, not sure. Laxepro. OK. Um, that's fine. Any other medication that you're using? Over the counter, anything at all? <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK. Right. Any allergies?"] No. Nothing, yeah, nothing.

[Clinician asked: "No allergies at all, righty . Fine so, obviously, you know you just have diarrhea it's about four to five times a day. Um, you're still drinking plenty and you look like you're not dehydrated which is a good sign, OK? Um, when we start worrying about diarrhea is if you're going more than eight times"] Mmm.

[Clinician asked: "OK, when you go to the toilet. Um, things that one would advise is a soft diet. So avoid dairy, fish, meat, eggs, while you're having the diarrhea. Go, sorry?"] Soft . What is a soft diet? What does that ? Like, outside of, not those things, what do you mean by soft?

[Clinician asked: "OK, so that will be like crackers, toast, jam, those kind of things that's easily digested. Like what we are good give babies yeah. When they start eating you give, simple food for babies. You don't give eggs or meat the first time they start feeding. So that's the kind of soft diet we're talking ab"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> What do you mean OK with that diet, like once?

[Clinician asked: "Forty-eight hours you're not having any diarrhea, you're fine then you can go and start having meat, egg, fish and all. So that <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>."] OK,  if if the diarrhoea is getting better but isn't getting, or do you want it to go away completely at that point, or if it's like trending upwards?

[Clinician asked: "No, no, no  preferably just don't because, what why do we say this? It's because it takes much more time for meat to get digested. So because your gut flora, you know, the, the, the bacteria in your gut is not you know as it should be, it takes a bit more effort for one to digest these food. While i"] Yes.

[Clinician asked: "Yeah? So, um, at the same time you could have some, although you can't  milk is not a good idea, yoghurts are a good idea because they are probiotics, it help to build up the normal, um gut, gut bacteria back. OK."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "So those kinda, now, things to look out for, if your diarrhoea doesn't go away in a week's time, or if you're passing blood, we need a stool sample, because that means you  you may have , um, some particular bugs that might need treatment with antibiotics."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "OK. If you're again, as I've mentioned previously if you're going about six to eight times a day, that means there's a high chance that, uh, you are, having  dehydration and you might need to go into hospital at that point in time. If that happens you get in touch with us again, all right?"] OK.
[Clinician asked: "Any questions? You look a bit confused."] No, that's it.

[Clinician asked: "Yeah? OK and also you could get something called Dioralyte over the counter. So, it helps to uh, replenish your, the salts that you've been, that's lost through going to the toilet."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "OK? If you're a bit confused you know you can actually, uh, all this is all recorded in your app. So you can go back. I'll put that all in detail so if, you know I know sometimes it's a bit difficult to process everything, you can go back and read, read the notes."] Perfect, that's great.
[Clinician asked: "Is that OK? All right then, you take care."] Bye.
gold_note:
Diarrhoea for 3 days 
Also associated abdominal pain 
No mucous, no blood 
Vomiting once 
Still eating and drinking 
Diarrhea like water 4-5 times a day 
No fever 
Loss od appetite 
Did not eat out 
No foreign travel 
No dehydration on examination 
PMH : asthma 
FH: Dad died of bowel cancer 10 years, HTN
Medication ? Lapriso
No allergies 
Diagnosis: Gastroenteritis
Plan : advised soft diet , drink plenty . Start with crackers, soups and mash . Then can build on with mash and vegetable. Can use yoghurts as probiotics and will help build the gut. If the diarhoea goes on for more than a week or there is blood , we would need a stool sample. 
If there is diarrhea more than 8 episodes, you may get dehydrated please book an appointment.

gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
Diarrhoea
PMH : asthma
Dad died of bowel cancer 10 years HTN
Gastroenteritis

naive.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It sounds like you're experiencing a bout of diarrhea that's been going on for a few days, which can be quite uncomfortable. I'm sorry you're feeling this way, and I want to make sure you have the information you need to feel better soon.

To help manage your symptoms, I recommend sticking to a soft diet, which includes foods like toast, crackers, and bananas, to avoid aggravating your digestive system further. It's important to stay well-hydrated, especially since you've been experiencing vomiting. You might consider taking an over-the-counter rehydration solution like Dioralyte to help replenish any lost fluids and electrolytes.

If your symptoms don't improve within a week, or if you start experiencing more severe symptoms such as blood in your stool, high fever, or if you're unable to keep fluids down, please don't hesitate to reach out or seek further medical attention. These could be signs of a more serious issue that needs to be addressed.

In the meantime, rest and take care of yourself. If you have any more questions or concerns, feel free to contact me. I'm here to help you through this.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thanks for sharing all that with me, Michael. It makes sense you're feeling concerned, especially with the diarrhea lasting for three days and the vomiting. I can see how this would be unsettling, especially given your family history of bowel cancer. I want to make sure we address this together and get you feeling better soon.

A few things stand out to me. First, the duration of the diarrhea and the vomiting are important. While it doesn’t sound like you have any alarming symptoms like blood or severe pain, it’s worth keeping an eye on. Diarrhea can sometimes be caused by infections, food intolerances, or even stress, but given your family history, I’ll want to make sure we rule out anything more serious. I’ll suggest starting with a soft diet to help your gut recover and staying well-hydrated, especially since you’re able to keep fluids down.

If the diarrhea doesn’t improve within a week, or if you start experiencing more severe symptoms like abdominal pain, fever, or dehydration (like dry mouth, reduced urination, or dizziness), it might be time to get some tests done, like a stool sample or blood work, to check for infections or other causes. In the meantime, over-the-counter rehydration solutions like Dioralyte can help replenish lost fluids and electrolytes.

Before we wrap up, could you summarize for me what we’ve agreed on? That way, I can make sure everything’s clear and you feel confident in the plan. We’ll check in again in a week to see how you’re doing, but feel free to reach out sooner if things get worse.

Let's plan to see each other again in a week. In the meantime, if your symptoms worsen or if you experience any severe pain, please call us right away.
